1:
"Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the commandment of God our Saviour, and Lord Jesus Christ, which is our hope;" The witnesses note the apostolic salutation grounded in God's commandment. Chrysostom notes the dignity of an apostle is great and admirable, and Paul constantly sets forth its cause, not as taking the honour to himself, but as intrusted with it and under necessity — "a necessity is laid upon me." The witnesses note Paul opening the letter as an apostle of Jesus Christ by the commandment of God our Saviour and the Lord Jesus Christ, who is our hope; the apostleship by divine command and the titles of God and Christ — that Paul grounds his office not in his own will but in the express "commandment" of God, marking that his apostleship was laid on him by divine appointment; and he names God as "our Saviour" and the Lord Jesus Christ as "our hope," so that the very salutation carries rich doctrine — God the author of salvation, and Christ himself the ground and substance of the believer's hope; an apostle by the commandment of God our Saviour. The witnesses note an apostle of Jesus Christ by the commandment of God our Saviour. The witnesses note Lord Jesus Christ, which is our hope.

The witnesses note Paul an apostle by God's commandment — naming God "our Saviour" and Christ "our hope"; the salutation grounded in divine appointment.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostleship by divine command. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul's office rests on God's express commandment, with God named Saviour and Christ our hope. The witnesses note Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the apostle's dignity, taken not to himself but intrusted, by necessity; the dignity; the not to himself; the intrusted; the necessity; the office. The expositors observe the by commandment: "by the commandment of God our Saviour" — by the express commandment of God; the by commandment; the express; the divine appointment; the laid on him; the office. The witnesses press the God our Saviour: God is named "our Saviour" — the author of salvation; the God our Saviour; the the author; the salvation; the title; the named. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ our hope: "and Lord Jesus Christ, which is our hope" — Christ is our hope, the ground and substance; the Christ our hope; the the ground; the the substance; the the believer's hope; the named. The witnesses observe the divine appointment: Paul's apostleship was laid on him by divine appointment, not his own will; the divine appointment; the not his will; the laid on; the office; the called. The expositors note the rich doctrine: the salutation carries rich doctrine — God Saviour, Christ our hope; the rich doctrine; the God Saviour; the Christ our hope; the the salutation; the doctrine. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the an apostle by the commandment of God our Saviour, the Lord Jesus Christ which is our hope, and the apostleship by command. The verse thus records the apostleship by divine command — teaching that Paul's office rests on God's express commandment, with God named Saviour and Christ our hope.
2:
"Unto Timothy, my own son in the faith: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord." The witnesses note the address to Timothy and the threefold benediction. Gill notes Timothy was Paul's own son in the faith, not in the flesh or by natural descent, but in a spiritual sense — though Paul was not properly his spiritual father (Timothy being already converted), the relation was spiritual, of one in the faith of Christ. The witnesses note Paul addressing Timothy as his own son in the faith, with the benediction of grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Jesus Christ; the tender bond and the fuller blessing to a fellow-minister — that Paul greets Timothy as a true spiritual son, the affection of a father in the gospel, and invokes upon him not the usual "grace and peace" only, but adds "mercy" between them — a fuller blessing fitting for a minister of the gospel, who has special need of God's mercy in his great work — all flowing from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together; grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord. The witnesses note Timothy, my own son in the faith. The witnesses note grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.

The witnesses note Paul greeting Timothy as his own son in the faith, with grace, mercy, and peace — the added "mercy" fitting for a minister's special need.

So the witnesses make the verse the address and benediction to Timothy. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender father-son bond and the fuller blessing, with "mercy" added, fitting a minister. The witnesses note the own son: "Timothy, my own son in the faith" — Gill notes Timothy was Paul's son in a spiritual sense; the own son; the in the faith; the spiritual; the not the flesh; the bond. The expositors observe the spiritual bond: the relation was spiritual, of one in the faith of Christ; the spiritual bond; the one in the faith; the of Christ; the the relation; the affection. The witnesses press the grace, mercy, peace: "grace, mercy, and peace" — the threefold benediction, with "mercy" added; the grace mercy peace; the threefold; the mercy added; the the blessing; the fuller. The expositors draw the lesson on the mercy: "mercy" is added, fitting for a minister's special need; the mercy added; the the minister; the special need; the the work; the fuller. The witnesses observe the from the Father and Christ: "from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord" — flowing from the Father and the Lord Jesus together; the from the Father; the and Christ; the together; the the fountain; the joint. The expositors note the affection: Paul greets Timothy with a father's affection in the gospel; the affection; the a father; the in the gospel; the tender; the son. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Timothy my own son in the faith, the grace mercy and peace from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord, and the benediction. The verse thus records the address and benediction to Timothy — teaching the tender father-son bond and the fuller blessing, with "mercy" added, fitting a minister.
3:
"As I besought thee to abide still at Ephesus, when I went into Macedonia, that thou mightest charge some that they teach no other doctrine," The witnesses note the charge to Timothy to silence false teaching at Ephesus. Barnes notes it is clear that Paul and Timothy had been labouring together at Ephesus, and that Paul had been compelled to leave before he had completed what he wished, leaving Timothy to carry it on. The witnesses note Paul reminding Timothy that he had besought him to remain at Ephesus, when Paul went into Macedonia, that he might charge some to teach no other doctrine; the commission to guard the church against false doctrine — that Paul recalls the reason he had left Timothy at Ephesus: not for idleness, but with a solemn charge to "charge some" — the false teachers troubling the church — "that they teach no other doctrine" than the apostolic gospel, so that Timothy's first and chief task was the guarding of sound doctrine and the silencing of error; that thou mightest charge some that they teach no other doctrine. The witnesses note I besought thee to abide still at Ephesus. The witnesses note that thou mightest charge some that they teach no other doctrine.

The witnesses note the charge to Timothy at Ephesus — to charge the false teachers to teach no other doctrine; his chief task the guarding of sound doctrine.

So the witnesses make the verse the commission to guard against false doctrine. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy was left at Ephesus to charge the false teachers and guard the apostolic gospel. The witnesses note the abide: "I besought thee to abide still at Ephesus" — Paul besought Timothy to remain at Ephesus; the abide; the at Ephesus; the remain; the left; the besought. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes Paul had to leave before completing the work, leaving Timothy; the had to leave; the completing; the leaving Timothy; the carry on; the the work. The witnesses press the charge some: "that thou mightest charge some" — to charge some (the false teachers); the charge some; the the false teachers; the troubling; the the church; the silence. The expositors draw the lesson on the no other doctrine: "that they teach no other doctrine" — to teach no other doctrine than the apostolic gospel; the no other doctrine; the the apostolic gospel; the sound; the the truth; the guard. The witnesses observe the guarding: Timothy's task was the guarding of sound doctrine; the guarding; the sound doctrine; the his task; the chief; the guard. The expositors note the silencing error: and the silencing of error; the silencing error; the error; the false; the silence; the charge. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I besought thee to abide still at Ephesus, the that thou mightest charge some that they teach no other doctrine, and the commission to guard. The verse thus records the commission to guard against false doctrine — teaching that Timothy was left at Ephesus to charge the false teachers and guard the gospel.
4:
"Neither give heed to fables and endless genealogies, which minister questions, rather than godly edifying which is in faith: so do." The witnesses note the warning against fables and endless genealogies. Gill notes the "fables" are old wives' or Jewish fables, the traditions of the elders — anything not true, or, if true, yet idle, vain, trifling, and unprofitable. Barnes notes they were probably the idle and puerile superstitions of the Jews. The witnesses note Paul charging that Timothy give no heed to fables and endless genealogies, which minister questions rather than godly edifying in faith; the rejection of vain speculation in favour of edifying faith — that Paul specifies what the false teachers peddled: "fables" (the unprofitable myths and traditions of the Jews) and "endless genealogies" (interminable tracing of descents and speculations), which "minister questions" (breed disputes and idle controversies) rather than the true "godly edifying which is in faith" — the building up of souls in the faith — so that Timothy is to turn from barren speculation to what truly edifies; fables and endless genealogies, which minister questions. The witnesses note neither give heed to fables and endless genealogies. The witnesses note which minister questions, rather than godly edifying which is in faith.

The witnesses note the warning against fables and endless genealogies — which breed idle disputes rather than the godly edifying which is in faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the rejection of vain speculation. The lesson the expositors draw is that fables and genealogies breed disputes, while faith edifies, so Timothy turns from speculation to what builds up. The witnesses note the fables: "neither give heed to fables" — Gill notes old wives' or Jewish fables, idle and unprofitable; the fables; the Jewish; the idle; the unprofitable; the vain. The expositors observe the genealogies: "and endless genealogies" — endless genealogies (interminable tracing of descents); the genealogies; the endless; the descents; the speculations; the interminable. The witnesses press the minister questions: "which minister questions" — which breed disputes and idle controversies; the minister questions; the disputes; the controversies; the idle; the breed. The expositors draw the lesson on the godly edifying: "rather than godly edifying which is in faith" — rather than the godly edifying in faith (building up souls); the godly edifying; the in faith; the building up; the souls; the edify. The witnesses observe the barren speculation: Timothy is to turn from barren speculation; the barren speculation; the turn from; the vain; the unprofitable; the speculation. The expositors note the what edifies: to what truly edifies in the faith; the what edifies; the truly; the in the faith; the builds up; the edify. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the neither give heed to fables and endless genealogies, the which minister questions rather than godly edifying which is in faith, and the rejection of speculation. The verse thus records the rejection of vain speculation — teaching that fables and genealogies breed disputes, while faith edifies.
5:
"Now the end of the commandment is charity out of a pure heart, and of a good conscience, and of faith unfeigned:" The witnesses note that the aim of the commandment is love from a pure heart. Chrysostom notes nothing is so injurious as to undervalue friendship and love, nor so beneficial as to pursue it; Christ showed this, and Paul, against the wranglers, sets up love as the very end and scope of all the commandment. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the end of the commandment is charity out of a pure heart, a good conscience, and faith unfeigned; the goal of all teaching is love, springing from a pure heart, good conscience, and sincere faith — that Paul sets against the false teachers' barren wrangling the true "end" (aim, design, scope) of the whole commandment and gospel charge: not vain disputes, but "charity" (love to God and man) — and that love flowing from three holy springs: "a pure heart" (cleansed and renewed), "a good conscience" (free from guilt and hypocrisy), and "faith unfeigned" (a real, sincere faith) — so that the whole of true religion comes to flower in love born of a cleansed and believing heart; charity out of a pure heart, and of a good conscience, and of faith unfeigned. The witnesses note the end of the commandment is charity. The witnesses note out of a pure heart, and of a good conscience, and of faith unfeigned.

The witnesses note the end of the commandment is love — from a pure heart, a good conscience, and faith unfeigned; the goal of all teaching set against barren wrangling.

So the witnesses make the verse the goal of love from a pure heart. The lesson the expositors draw is that the aim of all the commandment is love springing from a cleansed and believing heart, not vain disputes. The witnesses note the end: "the end of the commandment is charity" — Chrysostom notes Paul sets up love as the end of all the commandment; the end; the charity; the love; the the aim; the scope. The expositors observe the against wrangling: love is set against the false teachers' barren wrangling; the against wrangling; the the false teachers; the barren; the disputes; the love. The witnesses press the pure heart: "out of a pure heart" — love from a pure heart (cleansed, renewed); the pure heart; the cleansed; the renewed; the the spring; the heart. The expositors draw the lesson on the good conscience: "and of a good conscience" — and a good conscience (free from guilt and hypocrisy); the good conscience; the free from guilt; the no hypocrisy; the the spring; the conscience. The witnesses observe the faith unfeigned: "and of faith unfeigned" — and faith unfeigned (real, sincere); the faith unfeigned; the real; the sincere; the the spring; the faith. The expositors note the three springs: love flows from these three holy springs; the three springs; the pure heart; the good conscience; the faith; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the end of the commandment is charity, the out of a pure heart and of a good conscience and of faith unfeigned, and the goal of love. The verse thus records the goal of love from a pure heart — teaching that the aim of all the commandment is love springing from a cleansed and believing heart.
6:
"From which some having swerved have turned aside unto vain jangling;" The witnesses note the false teachers who swerved from love into empty talk. Gill notes the apostle describes the persons he suspected of teaching other doctrines and introducing fables and genealogies — such who departed from love, a pure heart, good conscience, and unfeigned faith, missing the mark, the end and design of the commandment. The witnesses note Paul declaring that from these (love, a pure heart, good conscience, sincere faith) some, having swerved, have turned aside unto vain jangling; the departure from the true aim into empty wrangling — that Paul shows the sad result when the false teachers missed the true "end" of the commandment: having "swerved" (missed the mark, departed) from love and a pure heart and sincere faith, they "turned aside unto vain jangling" — empty, idle, and unprofitable talk, mere noise and dispute — so that, abandoning the goal of love, they fell into fruitless babbling; some having swerved have turned aside unto vain jangling. The witnesses note from which some having swerved. The witnesses note have turned aside unto vain jangling.

The witnesses note the false teachers who swerved from love into vain jangling — empty, idle talk; abandoning the goal of love, they fell into fruitless babbling.

So the witnesses make the verse the departure into empty wrangling. The lesson the expositors draw is that the false teachers, missing the true aim of love, fell into vain and fruitless talk. The witnesses note the swerved: "from which some having swerved" — Gill notes some departed from love, a pure heart, and faith; the swerved; the departed; the missed the mark; the from love; the turned. The expositors observe the missing the mark: they missed the mark, the end of the commandment; the missing the mark; the the end; the the commandment; the missed; the departed. The witnesses press the turned aside: "have turned aside" — turned aside (deviated); the turned aside; the deviated; the strayed; the from love; the departed. The expositors draw the lesson on the vain jangling: "unto vain jangling" — unto vain jangling (empty, idle talk); the vain jangling; the empty; the idle; the unprofitable; the babbling. The witnesses observe the empty talk: their talk was mere noise and dispute, fruitless; the empty talk; the noise; the dispute; the fruitless; the babbling. The expositors note the abandoning love: abandoning the goal of love, they fell into babbling; the abandoning love; the the goal; the fell; the babbling; the fruitless. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the from which some having swerved, the have turned aside unto vain jangling, and the departure into wrangling. The verse thus records the departure into empty wrangling — teaching that the false teachers, missing the true aim of love, fell into vain and fruitless talk.
7:
"Desiring to be teachers of the law; understanding neither what they say, nor whereof they affirm." The witnesses note the false teachers' presumption and ignorance. Gill notes they were fond of being called Rabbi and styled doctors of the law, of being thought to have skill in interpreting it and good talents in expounding it — which was now most in vogue, when the preaching of the gospel was treated with contempt. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they desire to be teachers of the law, understanding neither what they say nor whereof they affirm; the ambition of ignorant men to be law-teachers — that Paul exposes the false teachers' real character: they were filled with the ambition "to be teachers of the law" (craving the honour and title of doctors of the law, then in high vogue), yet were utterly incompetent for it, "understanding neither what they say" (not even grasping their own assertions) "nor whereof they affirm" (nor the subjects they so confidently pronounced upon) — empty pretenders, ignorant of the very thing they professed to teach; understanding neither what they say, nor whereof they affirm. The witnesses note desiring to be teachers of the law. The witnesses note understanding neither what they say, nor whereof they affirm.

The witnesses note the false teachers' presumption — craving to be teachers of the law, yet understanding neither what they say nor whereof they affirm; empty pretenders.

So the witnesses make the verse the ambition of ignorant men to teach. The lesson the expositors draw is that the false teachers, craving the title of law-teachers, were ignorant of the very thing they professed. The witnesses note the desiring: "desiring to be teachers of the law" — Gill notes they craved the title of doctors of the law; the desiring; the teachers of the law; the the title; the the honour; the craved. The expositors observe the in vogue: the title was in high vogue, the gospel held in contempt; the in vogue; the the title; the the gospel; the contempt; the high. The witnesses press the not understanding: "understanding neither what they say" — not even grasping their own assertions; the not understanding; the what they say; the their assertions; the not grasping; the ignorant. The expositors draw the lesson on whereof they affirm: "nor whereof they affirm" — nor the subjects they confidently pronounced on; the whereof they affirm; the the subjects; the confidently; the pronounced; the ignorant. The witnesses observe the empty pretenders: they were empty pretenders, ignorant of what they taught; the empty pretenders; the ignorant; the professed; the taught; the incompetent. The expositors note the incompetent: utterly incompetent for the office they craved; the incompetent; the the office; the craved; the unfit; the ignorant. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the desiring to be teachers of the law, the understanding neither what they say nor whereof they affirm, and the ambition of ignorant men. The verse thus records the ambition of ignorant men to teach — teaching that the false teachers, craving the title of law-teachers, were ignorant of the very thing they professed.
8:
"But we know that the law is good, if a man use it lawfully;" The witnesses note that the law is good when used lawfully. Gill notes Paul says this to prevent an objection — that because he bore hard on those fond of being teachers of the law, he was against the law; but he, his fellow labourers, and all believers know from Scripture that the law is good. The witnesses note Paul affirming that the law is good, if a man use it lawfully; the goodness of the law in its right use — that Paul, having rebuked the false law-teachers, guards against any charge that he despised the law itself: "we know that the law is good" — holy, just, and good in its own nature, as God's own — yet only "if a man use it lawfully" (in its proper, intended way), not as a means of justification (which it cannot give), but for the ends God designed, so that the fault lay not in the law but in its abuse by the ignorant teachers; the law is good, if a man use it lawfully. The witnesses note we know that the law is good. The witnesses note if a man use it lawfully.

The witnesses note the law is good in its right use — holy and good in itself, but only when used lawfully, not as a means of justification; the fault lay in its abuse.

So the witnesses make the verse the goodness of the law in its right use. The lesson the expositors draw is that the law is good in itself, the fault lying not in it but in its unlawful abuse. The witnesses note the law is good: "we know that the law is good" — Gill notes the law is good, as God's own; the law is good; the holy; the just; the God's own; the good. The expositors observe the guarding: Paul guards against the charge that he despised the law; the guarding; the the charge; the despised; the the law; the objection. The witnesses press the lawfully: "if a man use it lawfully" — only if used lawfully (in its proper way); the lawfully; the proper way; the right use; the intended; the lawful. The expositors draw the lesson on the not justification: the law is not used lawfully as a means of justification, which it cannot give; the not justification; the cannot give; the the law; the misused; the lawful. The witnesses observe the proper ends: the law is to be used for the ends God designed; the proper ends; the God designed; the the ends; the lawful; the intended. The expositors note the fault in the abuse: the fault lay not in the law but in its abuse by the ignorant; the fault in the abuse; the not the law; the the ignorant; the misused; the abuse. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we know that the law is good, the if a man use it lawfully, and the goodness of the law. The verse thus records the goodness of the law in its right use — teaching that the law is good in itself, the fault lying in its unlawful abuse.
9:
"Knowing this, that the law is not made for a righteous man, but for the lawless and disobedient, for the ungodly and for sinners, for unholy and profane, for murderers of fathers and murderers of mothers, for manslayers," The witnesses note that the law's coercive use is for the lawless, not the righteous. Gill notes that no man is naturally righteous since Adam, and none can be justified by the works of the law; only those are righteous who are made so through the imputation of Christ's righteousness — and for such the law is not made (as a terror). The witnesses note Paul declaring that the law is not made for a righteous man, but for the lawless, disobedient, ungodly, sinners, unholy, profane, and murderers; the law's restraining terror aimed at the wicked, not the justified — that Paul explains the lawful use of the law: it is "not made for a righteous man" (the believer, justified by Christ's righteousness, who is led by the Spirit and needs no lash of threat) but "for the lawless and disobedient" — and Paul names a fearful catalogue of the grossly wicked (the ungodly, profane, parricides, murderers) — against whom the law stands as a curb, a terror, and a condemnation, restraining and exposing their sin; the law is not made for a righteous man, but for the lawless and disobedient. The witnesses note the law is not made for a righteous man. The witnesses note but for the lawless and disobedient, for the ungodly and for sinners.

The witnesses note the law's coercive use aimed at the lawless, not the righteous — standing as a curb and terror against the wicked, restraining and exposing their sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the law's restraining use against the wicked. The lesson the expositors draw is that the law's terror is for the lawless, not the believer justified by Christ. The witnesses note the not for the righteous: "the law is not made for a righteous man" — Gill notes for the righteous (justified by Christ) the law is not made as a terror; the not for the righteous; the the justified; the by Christ; the no terror; the led by the Spirit. The expositors observe the for the lawless: "but for the lawless and disobedient" — but for the lawless and disobedient; the for the lawless; the disobedient; the the wicked; the the unruly; the against. The witnesses press the catalogue: "for the ungodly and for sinners, for unholy and profane, for murderers of fathers and murderers of mothers, for manslayers" — the catalogue of the grossly wicked; the catalogue; the ungodly; the profane; the parricides; the murderers. The expositors draw the lesson on the curb and terror: the law stands as a curb, a terror, and a condemnation against them; the curb; the terror; the condemnation; the against the wicked; the restrains. The witnesses observe the restraining sin: the law restrains and exposes their sin; the restraining; the exposing; the their sin; the curb; the terror. The expositors note the no lash for the believer: the believer, led by the Spirit, needs no lash of threat; the no lash; the the believer; the the Spirit; the no terror; the led. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the law is not made for a righteous man, the but for the lawless and disobedient for the ungodly and for sinners, and the law's terror for the wicked. The verse thus records the law's restraining use against the wicked — teaching that the law's terror is for the lawless, not the believer justified by Christ.
10:
"For whoremongers, for them that defile themselves with mankind, for menstealers, for liars, for perjured persons, and if there be any other thing that is contrary to sound doctrine;" The witnesses note the continuation of the catalogue and the standard of sound doctrine. Gill notes whoremongers (fornicators and adulterers) God will judge; Barnes notes "them that defile themselves with mankind" are sodomites, the crime that abounded in ancient times. The witnesses note Paul continuing the list — whoremongers, sodomites, menstealers, liars, perjured persons — and all that is contrary to sound doctrine; the further sins the law condemns and the test of sound teaching — that Paul completes his catalogue of the gross sins against which the law is directed (sexual immorality of various kinds, the man-stealing of slave-traders, lying, and perjury) and then, with a sweeping clause, adds "and if there be any other thing that is contrary to sound doctrine" — so that the standard of judgment is the "sound doctrine" of the gospel, and whatever opposes that wholesome teaching is condemned along with the rest; if there be any other thing that is contrary to sound doctrine. The witnesses note for whoremongers, for menstealers, for liars, for perjured persons. The witnesses note if there be any other thing that is contrary to sound doctrine.

The witnesses note the catalogue completed — sodomites, menstealers, liars, perjurers — with the sweeping standard: whatever is contrary to sound doctrine is condemned.

So the witnesses make the verse the further sins and the standard of sound doctrine. The lesson the expositors draw is that the law condemns these gross sins, and the test is the sound doctrine of the gospel. The witnesses note the whoremongers: "for whoremongers" — Gill notes fornicators and adulterers; the whoremongers; the fornicators; the adulterers; the immoral; the condemned. The expositors observe the sodomites: "for them that defile themselves with mankind" — Barnes notes sodomites; the sodomites; the defile with mankind; the the crime; the abounded; the condemned. The witnesses press the menstealers: "for menstealers, for liars, for perjured persons" — menstealers (slave-traders), liars, perjurers; the menstealers; the slave-traders; the liars; the perjurers; the condemned. The expositors draw the lesson on the sweeping clause: "and if there be any other thing that is contrary to sound doctrine" — and whatever opposes sound doctrine; the sweeping clause; the any other thing; the contrary; the sound doctrine; the condemned. The witnesses observe the standard: the standard of judgment is the sound doctrine of the gospel; the standard; the sound doctrine; the the gospel; the the test; the wholesome. The expositors note the whatever opposes: whatever opposes that wholesome teaching is condemned; the whatever opposes; the wholesome; the condemned; the the rest; the contrary. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for whoremongers for menstealers for liars for perjured persons, the if there be any other thing that is contrary to sound doctrine, and the standard of sound doctrine. The verse thus records the further sins and the standard of sound doctrine — teaching that the law condemns these gross sins, and the test is the sound doctrine of the gospel.
11:
"According to the glorious gospel of the blessed God, which was committed to my trust." The witnesses note that sound doctrine accords with the glorious gospel committed to Paul. Gill notes no doctrine is sound but what agrees with the gospel — a very great encomium of it: it is not only good news and glad tidings, but the gospel of God, of which he is the author, which relates to his glory, reveals his purposes, shows his covenant, and exhibits the blessings. The witnesses note Paul declaring that this sound doctrine is according to the glorious gospel of the blessed God, committed to his trust; the sound doctrine measured by the glorious gospel entrusted to Paul — that Paul names the standard by which all teaching is judged: "the glorious gospel of the blessed God" — a gospel called "glorious" because it displays all God's perfections and glory, and "of the blessed God" as its author — and he adds that this gospel "was committed to my trust," so that he holds it as a sacred deposit, a stewardship to be guarded and faithfully dispensed; the glorious gospel of the blessed God, which was committed to my trust. The witnesses note the glorious gospel of the blessed God. The witnesses note which was committed to my trust.

The witnesses note sound doctrine measured by the glorious gospel of the blessed God, committed to Paul's trust — a sacred deposit to be guarded and faithfully dispensed.

So the witnesses make the verse the glorious gospel as the standard. The lesson the expositors draw is that all teaching is measured by the glorious gospel of God, committed to Paul as a sacred trust. The witnesses note the glorious gospel: "the glorious gospel of the blessed God" — Gill notes the gospel is glorious, displaying God's glory; the glorious gospel; the glorious; the God's glory; the displays; the perfections. The expositors observe the of the blessed God: "of the blessed God" — God is its author, the blessed God; the of the blessed God; the the author; the blessed; the God; the source. The witnesses press the the standard: this gospel is the standard by which teaching is judged; the the standard; the judged; the sound doctrine; the measured; the the gospel. The expositors draw the lesson on the committed: "which was committed to my trust" — committed to Paul as a sacred deposit; the committed; the my trust; the the deposit; the sacred; the entrusted. The witnesses observe the stewardship: Paul holds it as a stewardship, to be guarded and dispensed; the stewardship; the guarded; the dispensed; the faithfully; the the trust. The expositors note the sacred trust: the gospel is a sacred trust laid on Paul; the sacred trust; the laid on; the Paul; the the gospel; the entrusted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the glorious gospel of the blessed God, the which was committed to my trust, and the gospel as standard. The verse thus records the glorious gospel as the standard — teaching that all teaching is measured by the glorious gospel of God, committed to Paul as a sacred trust.
12:
"And I thank Christ Jesus our Lord, who hath enabled me, for that he counted me faithful, putting me into the ministry;" The witnesses note Paul's thanks to Christ who enabled and trusted him for the ministry. Chrysostom notes the advantages of humility are acknowledged yet rarely met with; Paul cultivated it so that he is ever looking out for inducements to be humble, and even amid his great merits struggles to keep humble. The witnesses note Paul thanking Christ Jesus our Lord, who enabled him, counting him faithful and putting him into the ministry; the grateful acknowledgment of Christ's enabling grace and trust — that Paul, about to recall his own former sin, first breaks into thanksgiving to Christ for three things: that he "hath enabled me" (given him strength and gifts for the work), that "he counted me faithful" (judged and made him trustworthy), and that he was "putting me into the ministry" (placing so great a trust in his hands) — so that Paul owns his whole ministry, its power, its trust, and its very calling, as the gracious gift of Christ; I thank Christ Jesus our Lord, who hath enabled me. The witnesses note who hath enabled me, for that he counted me faithful. The witnesses note putting me into the ministry.

The witnesses note Paul's thanks to Christ who enabled him, counted him faithful, and put him into the ministry — owning his whole calling as the gift of Christ's grace.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's thanks for Christ's enabling grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul owns his whole ministry — its power, trust, and calling — as Christ's gracious gift. The witnesses note the thanks: "I thank Christ Jesus our Lord" — Paul thanks Christ; the thanks; the Christ; the our Lord; the gratitude; the praise. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes Paul's deep humility, looking for inducements to be humble; the humility; the inducements; the Paul; the humble; the deep. The witnesses press the enabled: "who hath enabled me" — Christ enabled him (gave strength and gifts); the enabled; the strength; the gifts; the for the work; the empowered. The expositors draw the lesson on counted faithful: "for that he counted me faithful" — Christ counted him faithful (made him trustworthy); the counted faithful; the trustworthy; the judged; the made; the trusted. The witnesses observe the the ministry: "putting me into the ministry" — placing him in the ministry (a great trust); the the ministry; the placing; the the trust; the the office; the calling. The expositors note the gracious gift: Paul owns his whole ministry as Christ's gracious gift; the gracious gift; the the ministry; the Christ's; the the calling; the owned. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who hath enabled me for that he counted me faithful, the putting me into the ministry, and the thanks for enabling grace. The verse thus records Paul's thanks for Christ's enabling grace — teaching that Paul owns his whole ministry as Christ's gracious gift.
13:
"Who was before a blasphemer, and a persecutor, and injurious: but I obtained mercy, because I did it ignorantly in unbelief." The witnesses note Paul's former sin and the mercy he obtained. Gill notes Paul was before a blasphemer of Christ's name, calling him an impostor and deceiver, and compelling others to blaspheme also; this and what follows is said to illustrate the grace of God in his conversion and call to the ministry. The witnesses note Paul confessing that he was before a blasphemer, persecutor, and injurious, but obtained mercy because he did it ignorantly in unbelief; the chief of sinners obtaining mercy in his ignorance — that Paul, to magnify the grace just owned, recalls his dark past: he was a "blasphemer" (reviling Christ and his name), a "persecutor" (violently hunting the church), and "injurious" (insolent and outrageous in his hostility) — yet "I obtained mercy" — and he adds the ground on which God dealt mercifully with him: "because I did it ignorantly in unbelief," not in the malice of a known truth resisted, but in the blindness of unbelief, so that his sin, though great, was not the unpardonable sin against light; but I obtained mercy, because I did it ignorantly in unbelief. The witnesses note who was before a blasphemer, and a persecutor, and injurious. The witnesses note I obtained mercy, because I did it ignorantly in unbelief.

The witnesses note Paul's dark past — blasphemer, persecutor, injurious — yet he obtained mercy, having done it ignorantly in unbelief, not against known truth.

So the witnesses make the verse the mercy shown to the former persecutor. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul, though a blasphemer and persecutor, obtained mercy in his ignorant unbelief, magnifying grace. The witnesses note the blasphemer: "who was before a blasphemer" — Gill notes Paul reviled Christ's name and compelled others; the blasphemer; the reviled; the Christ's name; the compelled; the past. The expositors observe the persecutor and injurious: "and a persecutor, and injurious" — a persecutor (hunting the church) and injurious (insolent, outrageous); the persecutor; the injurious; the hunting; the insolent; the hostile. The witnesses press the obtained mercy: "but I obtained mercy" — yet he obtained mercy; the obtained mercy; the yet; the mercy; the spared; the grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the ignorance: "because I did it ignorantly in unbelief" — because he did it ignorantly in unbelief; the ignorance; the unbelief; the ignorantly; the blindness; the not malice. The witnesses observe the not against light: not in the malice of known truth resisted, but in the blindness of unbelief; the not against light; the not malice; the known truth; the blindness; the unbelief. The expositors note the magnifying grace: the confession magnifies the grace just owned; the magnifying grace; the the confession; the grace; the owned; the illustrate. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who was before a blasphemer and a persecutor and injurious, the I obtained mercy because I did it ignorantly in unbelief, and the mercy to the persecutor. The verse thus records the mercy shown to the former persecutor — teaching that Paul, though a blasphemer, obtained mercy in his ignorant unbelief, magnifying grace.
14:
"And the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant with faith and love which is in Christ Jesus." The witnesses note the exceeding abundance of the grace shown to Paul. Gill notes the grace here is the love of God; God is abundant in grace and goodness, rich and plenteous in mercy, and there is an overflow of love in his heart to his chosen, which in conversion flows out and abounds and superabounds. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant, with faith and love in Christ Jesus; the superabounding grace that brought faith and love where sin had reigned — that Paul crowns his confession by magnifying the grace that met his great sin: "the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant" — not merely abundant, but overflowing and superabounding, more than equal to his guilt — and that grace came "with faith and love which is in Christ Jesus," so that where blasphemy and hatred had ruled, grace planted the very opposite graces, the faith that believes in Christ and the love that cleaves to him, both flowing from him as their source; the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant. The witnesses note the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant. The witnesses note with faith and love which is in Christ Jesus.

The witnesses note the superabounding grace shown to Paul — more than equal to his guilt — bringing faith and love in Christ where blasphemy and hatred had ruled.

So the witnesses make the verse the superabounding grace to Paul. The lesson the expositors draw is that grace overflowed and superabounded over Paul's sin, planting faith and love where hatred had ruled. The witnesses note the abundant: "the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant" — Gill notes grace abounded and superabounded; the abundant; the exceeding; the overflowing; the superabounding; the grace. The expositors observe the over the sin: the grace was more than equal to his guilt; the over the sin; the more than equal; the his guilt; the superabounding; the grace. The witnesses press the faith and love: "with faith and love which is in Christ Jesus" — with faith and love in Christ; the faith and love; the in Christ; the planted; the graces; the opposite. The expositors draw the lesson on the opposite graces: where blasphemy and hatred ruled, grace planted faith and love; the opposite graces; the blasphemy; the hatred; the faith; the love. The witnesses observe the in Christ the source: faith and love flow from Christ as their source; the in Christ; the the source; the flow; the from him; the graces. The expositors note the crowning the confession: this crowns Paul's confession, magnifying grace; the crowning; the the confession; the magnifying; the grace; the climax. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the grace of our Lord was exceeding abundant, the with faith and love which is in Christ Jesus, and the superabounding grace. The verse thus records the superabounding grace to Paul — teaching that grace overflowed over his sin, planting faith and love where hatred had ruled.
15:
"This is a faithful saying, and worthy of all acceptation, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners; of whom I am chief." The witnesses note the faithful saying of Christ's coming to save sinners. Chrysostom notes that God's favours so far exceed human hope and expectation that often they are not believed; so the apostles spend much discourse securing belief of the gifts granted us, as a man receiving a great good asks if it is not a dream. The witnesses note Paul declaring it a faithful saying, worthy of all acceptation, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom he is chief; the heart of the gospel sealed and Paul's own place as chief of sinners — that Paul gathers the whole gospel into one weighty, trustworthy maxim — "Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners" — sealing it as "a faithful saying" (certainly true) and "worthy of all acceptation" (to be embraced by all), so that the purpose of Christ's coming was the saving of sinners as such; and Paul, far from excepting himself, claims the lowest place — "of whom I am chief" — counting himself the foremost of sinners saved, so that his own case becomes the great proof of the saying's truth; Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners; of whom I am chief. The witnesses note Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. The witnesses note of whom I am chief.

The witnesses note the faithful saying — "Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners; of whom I am chief" — the gospel's heart sealed, Paul the foremost example.

So the witnesses make the verse the faithful saying of salvation for sinners. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ came to save sinners, and Paul, chief of sinners, is the great proof of the saying. The witnesses note the faithful saying: "this is a faithful saying, and worthy of all acceptation" — a trustworthy maxim, to be embraced by all; the faithful saying; the trustworthy; the worthy; the acceptation; the embraced. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes God's favours exceed expectation, so the apostles secure belief; the the favours; the exceed; the secure belief; the gifts; the assurance. The witnesses press the came to save sinners: "that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners" — Christ came to save sinners as such; the came to save; the sinners; the the purpose; the the world; the salvation. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel's heart: this is the whole gospel in one maxim; the gospel's heart; the one maxim; the whole gospel; the gathered; the the saying. The witnesses observe the chief of sinners: "of whom I am chief" — Paul claims the lowest place, chief of sinners; the chief; the lowest place; the foremost; the of sinners; the himself. The expositors note the great proof: Paul's own case is the great proof of the saying; the great proof; the his case; the the saying; the proof; the truth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, the of whom I am chief, and the faithful saying. The verse thus records the faithful saying of salvation for sinners — teaching that Christ came to save sinners, and Paul, chief of sinners, is the great proof of the saying.
16:
"Howbeit for this cause I obtained mercy, that in me first Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering, for a pattern to them which should hereafter believe on him to life everlasting." The witnesses note that Paul's pardon was made a pattern for all believers. Barnes notes this was one of the causes, a leading reason, of his pardon — not the only one, for God had other ends; but this was a design, that there might be for all ages a permanent proof that sins of the deepest dye may be forgiven. The witnesses note Paul declaring that for this cause he obtained mercy, that in him first Jesus might show all longsuffering as a pattern for those who should hereafter believe; the pardon of the chief sinner set as a permanent pattern of hope — that Paul, having owned himself chief of sinners, sees in his own conversion a divine design: God obtained mercy for him "first" (as a foremost or pre-eminent instance) "that in me... Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering" — displaying the full extent of his patient mercy on the worst of men — "for a pattern" (a sample, an encouraging example) to all who should "hereafter believe on him to life everlasting," so that no sinner, however great, need despair, the chief of sinners being saved to assure the rest; that in me first Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering, for a pattern. The witnesses note in me first Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering. The witnesses note for a pattern to them which should hereafter believe.

The witnesses note Paul's pardon set as a pattern — that in him, the chief, Christ might show all longsuffering, a sample of hope so no great sinner need despair.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's pardon as a pattern of hope. The lesson the expositors draw is that God saved the chief of sinners as a permanent example, that no sinner need despair. The witnesses note the for this cause: "for this cause I obtained mercy" — Barnes notes a leading reason of his pardon; the for this cause; the obtained mercy; the the reason; the design; the pardon. The expositors observe the in me first: "that in me first Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering" — in him first (foremost) Christ showed all longsuffering; the in me first; the foremost; the all longsuffering; the patient mercy; the displayed. The witnesses press the all longsuffering: Christ displayed the full extent of his patient mercy on the worst; the all longsuffering; the the full extent; the the worst; the patient mercy; the displayed. The expositors draw the lesson on the pattern: "for a pattern to them which should hereafter believe" — for a pattern (sample, example) to all who should believe; the pattern; the sample; the example; the believe; the encouraging. The witnesses observe the no despair: so that no sinner, however great, need despair; the no despair; the no sinner; the however great; the assured; the hope. The expositors note the permanent proof: a permanent proof that the deepest sins may be forgiven; the permanent proof; the the deepest sins; the forgiven; the all ages; the proof. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the in me first Jesus Christ might shew forth all longsuffering, the for a pattern to them which should hereafter believe, and the pardon as pattern. The verse thus records Paul's pardon as a pattern of hope — teaching that God saved the chief of sinners as a permanent example, that no sinner need despair.
17:
"Now unto the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only wise God, be honour and glory for ever and ever. Amen." The witnesses note the doxology to the eternal King and only wise God. Gill notes this doxology may refer to all three divine Persons, Father, Son, and Spirit, who are the one and only God, to whom all the attributes of wisdom, power, eternity, immortality, and invisibility belong, and who are jointly concerned in the grace bestowed. The witnesses note Paul ascribing honour and glory for ever to the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only wise God; the outburst of praise to the glorious God on account of his grace — that Paul, overwhelmed by the grace shown to him, breaks into a great doxology, heaping up the incommunicable titles of God: "King eternal" (the everlasting Sovereign, of all ages), "immortal" (incorruptible, deathless), "invisible" (unseen by mortal eyes), "the only wise God" (alone possessing perfect wisdom) — and to this God ascribes "honour and glory for ever and ever," sealing it with "Amen," so that the contemplation of grace rises into the worship of the glorious Giver; the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only wise God, be honour and glory for ever. The witnesses note the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only wise God. The witnesses note be honour and glory for ever and ever. Amen.

The witnesses note the doxology to the King eternal, immortal, invisible, the only wise God — Paul's contemplation of grace rising into worship of the glorious Giver.

So the witnesses make the verse the doxology to the eternal King. The lesson the expositors draw is that the contemplation of grace rises into the worship of the glorious, eternal God. The witnesses note the King eternal: "the King eternal" — the everlasting Sovereign of all ages; the King eternal; the everlasting; the Sovereign; the of all ages; the king. The expositors observe the immortal, invisible: "immortal, invisible" — incorruptible, deathless, unseen; the immortal; the invisible; the incorruptible; the deathless; the unseen. The witnesses press the only wise God: "the only wise God" — alone possessing perfect wisdom; the only wise God; the alone; the perfect wisdom; the the only; the God. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour and glory: "be honour and glory for ever and ever" — to him be honour and glory for ever; the honour and glory; the for ever; the ascribed; the praise; the worship. The witnesses observe the outburst of praise: overwhelmed by grace, Paul breaks into praise; the outburst; the overwhelmed; the grace; the praise; the doxology. The expositors note Gill's note on the Persons: Gill notes the doxology may refer to all three Persons, the one God; the the Persons; the Father Son Spirit; the the one God; the the attributes; the jointly. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the King eternal immortal invisible the only wise God, the be honour and glory for ever and ever Amen, and the doxology. The verse thus records the doxology to the eternal King — teaching that the contemplation of grace rises into the worship of the glorious, eternal God.
18:
"This charge I commit unto thee, son Timothy, according to the prophecies which went before on thee, that thou by them mightest war a good warfare;" The witnesses note the solemn charge committed to Timothy, grounded in prophecy. Chrysostom notes the office of teacher and priest is of great dignity, and to bring forward one worthy requires a divine election; so it was of old and is now, when we choose without human passion, swayed by neither friendship nor enmity. The witnesses note Paul committing this charge to Timothy, according to the prophecies that went before on him, that he might war a good warfare; the ministry as a warfare entered by divine designation — that Paul, returning to his main purpose, solemnly "commits" (delivers as a sacred trust) "this charge" to Timothy his son, and reminds him that his calling to it was marked out "according to the prophecies which went before on thee" — the inspired utterances that had pointed him out for the work — so that, strengthened by the memory that God himself had designated him, Timothy might "war a good warfare," fighting the noble fight of faith against error and for the truth, as a soldier of Christ; that thou by them mightest war a good warfare. The witnesses note this charge I commit unto thee, son Timothy. The witnesses note that thou by them mightest war a good warfare.

The witnesses note the charge committed to Timothy, grounded in the prophecies that marked him out — that he might war a good warfare, the noble fight of faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the charge to war a good warfare. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy, designated by prophecy for the ministry, is to fight the noble fight of faith. The witnesses note the charge: "this charge I commit unto thee, son Timothy" — Paul commits the charge as a sacred trust; the charge; the commit; the sacred trust; the son Timothy; the delivered. The expositors observe the prophecies: "according to the prophecies which went before on thee" — the inspired utterances that pointed him out; the prophecies; the went before; the pointed out; the the work; the marked. The witnesses press Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the office requires a divine election; the divine election; the the office; the requires; the worthy; the designated. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine designation: God himself had designated Timothy for the work; the divine designation; the God; the designated; the the work; the marked out. The witnesses observe the war a good warfare: "that thou by them mightest war a good warfare" — that he might war a good warfare; the war a good warfare; the the fight; the noble; the of faith; the soldier. The expositors note the fight against error: the warfare is against error and for the truth; the against error; the for the truth; the the fight; the the warfare; the soldier of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this charge I commit unto thee son Timothy, the that thou by them mightest war a good warfare, and the charge to war. The verse thus records the charge to war a good warfare — teaching that Timothy, designated by prophecy, is to fight the noble fight of faith.
19:
"Holding faith, and a good conscience; which some having put away concerning faith have made shipwreck:" The witnesses note the holding of faith and a good conscience, and the shipwreck of those who cast them off. Gill notes "faith" here is the doctrine of faith, and the "holding" of it is not a mere profession, but a holding forth in the ministry of the word, in opposition to concealing or dropping it; joined to a good conscience, which keeps the doctrine pure. The witnesses note Paul charging Timothy to hold faith and a good conscience, which some having put away have made shipwreck of faith; the bond of sound doctrine and a good conscience, and the ruin of those who part them — that Paul names the twin things Timothy must keep in his warfare: "faith" (the sound doctrine of the gospel, held fast and held forth) and "a good conscience" (purity and integrity of heart and life), the two being closely bound, for a corrupt conscience soon corrupts the doctrine; and he warns by the example of those who, "having put away" a good conscience, have "made shipwreck" of the faith itself, the loss of holiness leading to the wreck of belief; holding faith, and a good conscience. The witnesses note holding faith, and a good conscience. The witnesses note which some having put away concerning faith have made shipwreck.

The witnesses note the holding of faith and a good conscience together — and the shipwreck of those who cast off conscience, the loss of holiness wrecking belief.

So the witnesses make the verse the bond of faith and a good conscience. The lesson the expositors draw is that sound doctrine and a good conscience are bound together, the casting off of one wrecking the other. The witnesses note the holding faith: "holding faith" — Gill notes faith is the doctrine, held fast and held forth; the holding faith; the the doctrine; the held fast; the held forth; the kept. The expositors observe the good conscience: "and a good conscience" — and a good conscience (purity, integrity); the good conscience; the purity; the integrity; the of heart; the holiness. The witnesses press the closely bound: the two are closely bound, conscience keeping the doctrine pure; the closely bound; the the two; the conscience; the keeps pure; the the doctrine. The expositors draw the lesson on the put away: "which some having put away" — some having put away a good conscience; the put away; the cast off; the the conscience; the abandoned; the some. The witnesses observe the shipwreck: "concerning faith have made shipwreck" — have made shipwreck of the faith; the shipwreck; the of the faith; the wrecked; the ruined; the lost. The expositors note the holiness and belief: the loss of holiness leads to the wreck of belief; the holiness and belief; the loss of holiness; the wreck; the belief; the corruption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the holding faith and a good conscience, the which some having put away concerning faith have made shipwreck, and the bond of faith and conscience. The verse thus records the bond of faith and a good conscience — teaching that sound doctrine and a good conscience are bound together, the casting off of one wrecking the other.
20:
"Of whom is Hymenaeus and Alexander; whom I have delivered unto Satan, that they may learn not to blaspheme." The witnesses note the naming of two who were delivered to Satan. Gill notes Hymenaeus made shipwreck of the doctrine of the resurrection of the dead, by which the faith of some was overthrown, attended with the putting away of a good conscience; and Alexander seems to be the same coppersmith who did Paul much evil. The witnesses note Paul naming Hymenaeus and Alexander, whom he had delivered to Satan that they might learn not to blaspheme; the apostolic discipline of two notorious errorists for their correction — that Paul gives two examples of those who had wrecked their faith: Hymenaeus (who denied or perverted the resurrection) and Alexander (likely the coppersmith who opposed Paul), whom Paul had "delivered unto Satan" — by his apostolic authority excommunicating them and giving them over to Satan's buffeting (perhaps with bodily affliction) — and that not for their destruction but their correction, "that they may learn not to blaspheme," the severe act aimed at bringing them to repentance; whom I have delivered unto Satan, that they may learn not to blaspheme. The witnesses note of whom is Hymenaeus and Alexander. The witnesses note whom I have delivered unto Satan, that they may learn not to blaspheme.

The witnesses note Hymenaeus and Alexander delivered to Satan — excommunicated by apostolic authority, not for destruction but that they might learn not to blaspheme.

So the witnesses make the verse the discipline of two errorists. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul delivered the two to Satan for their correction, that they might learn not to blaspheme. The witnesses note the two: "of whom is Hymenaeus and Alexander" — Gill notes Hymenaeus wrecked the resurrection doctrine, Alexander likely the coppersmith; the two; the Hymenaeus; the Alexander; the the resurrection; the the coppersmith. The expositors observe the delivered to Satan: "whom I have delivered unto Satan" — Paul delivered them to Satan (by apostolic authority); the delivered to Satan; the apostolic authority; the excommunicating; the given over; the buffeting. The witnesses press the not destruction: not for their destruction but their correction; the not destruction; the correction; the not ruin; the their good; the aim. The expositors draw the lesson on the learn not to blaspheme: "that they may learn not to blaspheme" — that they might learn not to blaspheme; the learn not to blaspheme; the correction; the repentance; the brought; the aim. The witnesses observe the severe act for good: the severe act aimed at bringing them to repentance; the severe act; the for good; the repentance; the brought; the discipline. The expositors note the apostolic discipline: this is apostolic discipline of notorious errorists; the apostolic discipline; the errorists; the notorious; the discipline; the correction. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of whom is Hymenaeus and Alexander, the whom I have delivered unto Satan that they may learn not to blaspheme, and the discipline of errorists. The verse thus records the discipline of two errorists — teaching that Paul delivered them to Satan for their correction, that they might learn not to blaspheme.
